1: A theme: the conditional "if it had not been the LORD who was on our side" is the premise of the entire gospel. Romans 8:31 — "If God is for us, who can be against us?" The rhetorical question that Paul raises is the confident answer to the psalm's conditional — in Christ, the "if" has been resolved: God is for us, definitively and irreversibly, having given his own Son (Romans 8:32).
2: A theme: YHWH on Israel's side when enemies rose against them — the pattern of divine solidarity that reaches its ultimate expression in the incarnation. God became flesh and stood with his people not just militarily but in death itself (Philippians 2:8 — "he humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death, even death on a cross").
3: A type: the mortal threat of being swallowed alive. Christ descended into the depths of death — Ephesians 4:9 ("he also descended into the lower, earthly regions") — and was "swallowed" by the grave. The threat Israel faced became the reality Christ entered, precisely so that the threat would be defeated. Acts 2:24 — "it was not possible for him to be held by death."
4: A type: the flood sweeping over, the torrent passing over. Christ was baptized into death itself (Mark 10:38-39 — his "baptism" he would undergo). The waters of judgment that threatened to overwhelm Israel were the waters that Christ descended into — fulfilling the pattern of Noah and Jonah (Matthew 12:40) — and the waters through which he brought his people safely to the other side (1 Peter 3:20-21).
5: A type: the raging waters. The Exodus pattern — escape through the sea — is the pattern Christ recapitulates. 1 Corinthians 10:1-2 — Israel was "baptized into Moses" in the cloud and sea; Christ is the greater Moses whose people are baptized into him (Romans 6:3-4), passing through death into resurrection life.
6: A shadow: "Blessed be the LORD, who has not given us as prey to their teeth!" Christ is the champion who defeats the devourer. 1 Peter 5:8 — Satan "prowls around like a roaring lion, seeking someone to devour." Hebrews 2:14-15 — Christ through death "destroyed the one who has the power of death, that is, the devil, and delivered all those who through fear of death were subject to lifelong slavery." The prey is not given over because Christ stood in the gap.
7: A theme: "we have escaped like a bird from the snare of the fowler; the snare is broken." Luke 4:18 — Christ came to "proclaim liberty to the captives." Galatians 5:1 — "For freedom Christ has set us free." The snare of sin and the law's condemnation is broken at the cross (Colossians 2:14-15 — "having nailed it to the cross"). The bird that escapes is the believer freed in Christ.
8: A direct: "Our help is in the name of the LORD, who made heaven and earth." Acts 4:12 — "there is salvation in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given among men by which we must be saved." The "name of the LORD" that is the help of his people is now known as the name of Jesus, the Lord and creator. Peter quotes this principle immediately after healing in Jesus's name (Acts 3:6,16).
